dcterms_spatial: Fannin County (Tex.)|Fort Smith (Ark.)|Cherokee Nation, Oklahoma|Fort Gibson (Indian Territory)
dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Sam B. Barnes, brother-in-law to Martha Albert, on her behalf for membership in the Cherokee Nation. A formerly enslaved person, he lived in Fannin County Texas before the Civil War and moved to Fort Smith after the war ended. He later migrated to the Cherokee Nation in Oklahoma, and married Martha Albert's younger sister, Jennie Boland. He also discusses the parents of Martha Albert and where they are buried.
dcterms_extent: 10 pages (5 page testimony with a copy)

dcterms_abstract: Letter from E.A. Hitchcock, Secretary of Interior, informing the Commission that the Department has granted a motion filed by attorneys Neal and London of Fort Smith, Arkansas to re-open the case of Martha Albert et al. Although her a applicaiton for enrollment as a Cherokee Freedman was previously denied, new evidence has emerged that suggests that Johnson Whitmire may have enslaved two women named Martha" in the Goingsnake District with divergent personal histories.

dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Martha Albert, a former enslaved African American, on her own behalf and her children for membership in the Cherokee Nation. She mentions that she lives in Tahlequah District, Oklahoma, but has been living in Sequoyah. Also includes testimonies from John Melton and nd Mandy Benton, both Cherokee Freedman. It is determined that Martha Albert appears in the "Kerns-Clifton" roll, but not in the 1880 tribal roll or the Census roll of 1896. Judgment is suspended and their names placed on a "doubtful card."

dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Aaron Whitmire, a Cherokee Freedman, on behalf of Martha Albert, a formerly enslaved person, for membership in the Cherokee Nation. Prior to the Civil War, Aaron had been enslaved by George Whitmire, the brother of Martha's owner, Johnson Whitmire, in Goingsnake District, He testifies as to whether Martha Albert and her mother Nancy had been sold to Dillingham, a man from Arkansas, who allegedly moved them to Texas. He also testifies as to whether another enslaved person with the name "Martha" had been living on the Whitmire farm at the time. Upon this additional testimony the Commission to the Five Civilized Tribes made a decision to file a motion for continuance.

dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Robert Barnes, a formerly enslaved person living in the the Sequoyah District in Oklahoma, on his own behalf, for enrollment as a Cherokee Freedman. Also includes the testimony of his uncle, Colly Albert from the Illinois Distrct, OKlahoma on his behalf, as well as the testimony of his aunt, Martha Albert from Tahlequah District, Oklahoma, on behalf of herself and her two children. According to his testimony, Robert Barnes is the son of Sam and Jennie Barnes. Jennie, the sister of Martha Albert, was a formerly enslaved person who had been enslaved by Johnson Whitmire.

dcterms_spatial: Saline District (Indian Territory)|Cherokee Nation, Oklahoma|Goingsnake District (Indian Territory)|Illinois DistrictCherokee Nation, Oklahoma|Sequoyah District (Indian Territory)|Fort Smith (Ark.)|Fort Gibson (Indian Territory)|Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma
dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Albert Johnson, Andy Crossland, Rose Rogers on behalf of Martha Albert, a formerly enslaved person, for membership in the Cherokee Nation. Albert Johnson, from Sequoyah District, testifies that he was a former neighbor of Martha Albert. Andy Crossland, a former enslaved person and Confederate soldier from the Illinois District, testifies that he lived in Sequoyah District before the Civil War. He recalls first meeting Martha at Cale Starr's plantation near Fort Smith, Arkansas. Rose Rogers, a formerly enslaved person from Saline District, testifies that she knew Martha when they were children in Goingsnake District before the Civil War, and that Martha and her family had been enslaved by Johnson Whitmire. She also testifes spending the Civil War in the Choctaw Nation.

dcterms_alternative: Application of Martha Albert et al. for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen
dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Ruth Hunt, a Cherokee Freedman from Vinita District, on behalf of Marth Albert, for membership in the Cherokee Nation. Ruth testifies that she spent the Civil War in Fort Smith, Arkansas, and that Martha's husband worked for her family in 1867 after she returned to the Cherokee Nation. Also includes the testimonies of Emma Finley, the sister of Ruth Hunt, from Tahlequah Territory, and Harry Still, a Cherokee Freedman and former enslaved person. According to his testimony, Harry Still had been previously owned by George Whitmire, the brother of Johnson Whitmire, Martha Albert's former enslaver.
dcterms_extent: 10 pages (5 page testimony with a copy)

dcterms_abstract: Testimony of Watt Whitmire on behalf of Martha Albert, a formerly enslaved person, for membership in the Cherokee Nation. Watt testifies that he is the son of Johnson Whitmire, Martha Albert's former enslaver. He discusses his recollections of enslaved persons living on his father's farm in Goingsnake District before the Civil War. Also includes the testimony of J.W. Alberty, a Cherokee "by blood" from Goingsnake District.. J.W. Alberty testifies about his recollection of the Whitmire family and the sale of Martha's family to a man called Dillingham from Arkansas, who allegedly later moved them to Texas.

dcterms_abstract: Testimony of J.W. Hughes on behalf of Martha Albert, a formerly enslaved person, for membership in the Cherokee Nation. According to his testimony, he lived in Sequoyah District in 1869, and was a neighbor of Martha Albert and her family. He discusses his recollections of what Martha's father had told him about the famiily's history and their move to Texas before the Civil War.
